english: beings, they might be denominated 'the (eighteen) specific blessings of humanity'. Often they are also used directly for 'condition of humanity, or of human nature', this kind of existence being, from a religious point of view, the best and most desirable. rnyed-dkáí dál-ba mi lus (rnyed dka'i dal ba mi lus), and similar expressions frq. occur (Cs. has calmness, tranquillity of mind, evidently mistaking it for rnal-byor (rnal 'byor)). dál-ba (dal ba), dál-bu (dal bu), dál-po (dal po), dál-mo (dal mo), W. also *dál-can* (*dal can*), quiet, calm, of the mind, the water; gentle, of the wind; slow, lazy; *bè-gyü'* dhál-wa (*bad rgyud* dal ba), or *bè-pa dhál-wa* (*bad pa dal ba*) C. phlegmatic disposition. — Adv. dál-bar (dal bar) (v. above), dál-gyis (dal gyis), dál-bus (dal bus), slowly, softly, gradually, e.g. to draw, opp to drág-tu (drag tu); dál-ₒgroi rgyun bžin (dal 'gro'i rgyun bzhin) like a stream flowing gently and softly; mi-dál-bar (mi dal bar) Dzl. incessantly.

(spelling dubious), *dal-tsón táṅ-ce* (*dal btsong gtong ce*) W. to carry on compulsory trade. This is frequently done by Eastern rulers, who in time of personal need make a sale of goods, compelling people to buy at fixed prices.
is said to be a provincialism, and secondary form of ɣži-ka-rtsé (gzhis ka rtse), n. of a town near Tashilunpo.
buzz, murmur, hum, low confused noise, as of crowds, of a number of praying people, of wailing prisoners, of birds on the wing Glr.
the Persian دیگ, a large kettle, washing-copper, brewer's copper.
1. Cs. a stammerer, also ḵa-dig (kha dig), cf. ₒdig-pa ('dig pa). — 2. C. reeling, staggering, intoxicated.
, gád-mo diṅ-diṅ (gad mo ding ding) Tar. 158, 4 prob. an onomatopoetic word, Schf. 'laughing aloud'.

1. num. fig.: 71. — 2. for tu (tu) (q.v.) after final ṅ (ng), d (d), n (n), m (m), r (r), l (l). — 3. how many? bslébs-nas zlá-ba du lon (bslebs nas zla ba du lon) how many months is it ago that he came? — du-dú (du du) how much, how many each time? dú-žig (du zhig) how much about? dú-ma (du ma) many, žag dú-ma (zhag du ma) many days; dú-mar (du mar) pýe (phye) it is divided into several (parts) Wdn.; lan dú-mar (lan du mar) many a time, often Cs.; *dú-ma rákša* (*du ma raksha*) C. col. a great many, very much (perh. 'devilishly much', from rākšas).

(cf. důd-pa (dud pa)) smoke, ₒtul ('thul), or gyén-du (gyen du) p̱yur (phyur) smoke rises Zam.; dú-ba-pa (du ba pa) Sp. very poor people that pay but a trifling tax, proletarians (prop. 'smoke-people' that have nothing but the smoke of their fire). — du-ba-mǰug-riṅ (du ba mjug ring) a comet. — du-žág (du zhag) C. the smoke or vapour hanging over towns and large villages in the morning.

poison, dug blúd-pa (dug blud pa) to administer a poisoned potion to a person, to give him poison to drink; dug-mi-ɣnód-par (dug mi gnod par) ₒgyur ('gyur) he becomes proof against poison Dom.; čú-la dug ₒdébs-pa (chu la dug 'debs pa) to poison the water Pth.; dug ɣsum (dug gsum) in a moral sense, ₒdod-čágs ('dod chags), žė-sdáṅ (zhe sdang); sometimes dug lṅá (dug lnga), five moral poisons, are mentioned. Comp. dúg-can (dug can) poisonous. — dug-ɣnyén (dug gnyen) an antidote Cs. — dug-mdá (dug mda') a poisoned arrow. — dug-sbrúl (dug sbrul) venomous serpent. — dug-méd (dug med) not poisonous. — dug-šóg (dug shog) poisonous paper Mil., Pth., Glr. — dug-sél (dug sel) that which neutralizes a poison Cs. — dug-srúṅ (dug srung) a preservative against poison Cs.
(or dúg-ste (dug ste)?) Ts., so, thus, in this manner, also núg-ti (nug ti).
, esp. Ü (= *ču-pa* (*chu pa*) Ts., *gon-če* (*gon chas*) W. coat, garment, dress Mil.

, esp. in medical writings; it seems to denote 1. heat: Tar. 31, 21 tsád-pai dugs-kyis (tsad pa'i dugs kyis) by the glowing heat of the day Schf.; S.g.: čuí dri dugs rláṅs-pa čé (chu'i dri dugs rlangs pa che) the water (i.e. urine) has a strong smell and emits much heat(?) and vapour; Lt. 22, 4. 5; 23, 4; 2S, 5; Se, 4; 2cV, 10. čuí rigs šin-tu dúgs-pa (chu'i rigs shin tu dugs pa) Mṅg. adj.? — 2. revenge, grudge, rancour, *dug kór-ce* (*dugs 'khor ce*), dugs-
